facts.title: Invention of Zero
facts.description: The Maya independently invented the mathematical concept of zero around 36 BCE, centuries before it was introduced to Europe. They used a shell-shaped glyph to represent it.

source.name: Encyclopedia Britannica
source.reference: Maya Mathematics and the Concept of Zero

facts.title: Base-20 System
facts.description: Unlike our base-10 (decimal) system, the Maya used a base-20 (vigesimal) number system, likely derived from counting on all fingers and toes.

facts.title: Advanced Hieroglyphics
facts.description: The Maya had the most sophisticated and highly developed writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas, consisting of hundreds of phonetic symbols and logograms.

facts.title: Bark Paper Books
facts.description: They wrote books called codices, made from the inner bark of fig trees. The pages were folded like an accordion and bound with jaguar skin or wooden boards.

facts.title: Tragic Loss of Literature
facts.description: During the Spanish conquest, Bishop Diego de Landa ordered the burning of countless Maya books in 1562. Today, only four known Maya codices remain in existence.

source.name: Yale University Art Gallery
source.reference: The Maya Codices and the Spanish Conquest

facts.title: Not Extinct
facts.description: A common misconception is that the Maya disappeared. In reality, millions of Maya descendants are alive today, primarily living in Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador.

facts.title: Living Languages
facts.description: There is no single 'Mayan language.' Today, there are over 20 distinct Mayan languages still spoken by over 6 million people.

facts.title: Complex Calendars
facts.description: The Maya used intertwining calendar systems, most notably the 260-day Tzolk'in (sacred calendar) and the 365-day Haab' (solar calendar), which combined to form a 52-year Calendar Round.

facts.title: The Long Count
facts.description: Their 'Long Count' calendar tracked vast amounts of time. The cycle that ended in December 2012 was simply the end of a b'ak'tun (a period of about 394 years), not a prediction of the world's end.

source.name: NASA
source.reference: Beyond 2012: Why the World Didn't End

facts.title: Tracking Venus
facts.description: Maya astronomers calculated the synodic period of Venus at 584 days, an astonishingly accurate measurement achieved entirely with the naked eye.

source.name: Archaeology Magazine
source.reference: The Maya Sense of Time and Astronomy

facts.title: No Wheels or Metal Tools
facts.description: The Maya built massive stone pyramids, palaces, and observatories using stone tools (like flint and obsidian) and immense human labor, without the use of metal tools or the wheel.

facts.title: Equinox Serpent
facts.description: At Chichen Itza, the Temple of Kukulcan (El Castillo) is designed so that during the spring and autumn equinoxes, the sun casts a shadow that looks like a giant snake slithering down the pyramid's steps.

facts.title: The Mesoamerican Ballgame
facts.description: The Maya played a high-stakes sport known as Pok-a-Tok using a heavy solid rubber ball. Players could only use their hips, thighs, and chests to keep the ball in the air.

facts.title: Sacrificial Sports
facts.description: The ballgame had deep religious significance. In some ceremonial instances, the losing team (or sometimes the winning captain) would be offered as a human sacrifice to the gods.

source.name: World History Encyclopedia
source.reference: The Maya Ballgame and Human Sacrifice

facts.title: Chocolate Currency
facts.description: Cacao beans were incredibly valuable in Maya society and were regularly used as a form of currency for trade and taxes.

source.name: Smithsonian Magazine
source.reference: The Maya and the History of Chocolate Currency

facts.title: Spicy Chocolate Drink
facts.description: The Maya did not eat solid chocolate bars. They consumed it as a bitter, frothy beverage mixed with chili peppers, water, and cornmeal, reserved mostly for the elite.

facts.title: Crossed Eyes
facts.description: Crossed eyes were considered a mark of profound beauty and nobility. Mothers would hang a bead or object between their babies' eyes to encourage them to cross permanently.

source.name: Michael D. Coe / Ancient Maya Literature

facts.title: Cranial Deformation
facts.description: Noble Maya families bound the heads of their infants between wooden boards to artificially elongate their skulls, resembling an ear of corn, a sacred crop.

facts.title: Jeweled Teeth
facts.description: Skilled Maya dentists used drills made of obsidian to bore holes into their teeth, which were then inlaid with precious stones like jade, turquoise, or pyrite for aesthetic appeal.

facts.title: Bloodletting Rituals
facts.description: Maya royals performed bloodletting rituals—piercing their tongues, lips, or genitals with stingray spines—believing their royal blood nourished the gods and kept the universe in balance.

facts.title: City-States
facts.description: The Maya never unified into a single empire like the Aztecs or Incas. Instead, they were a network of independent city-states that traded and frequently warred with one another.

facts.title: Advanced Water Management
facts.description: Because much of the Yucatan Peninsula lacks surface rivers, the Maya built sophisticated underground cisterns called 'chultunes' and massive reservoirs to catch and store rainwater.

facts.title: Sweat Baths
facts.description: The Maya frequently used stone sweat baths (similar to saunas), known as temazcals, to purify their bodies, treat illnesses, and perform spiritual cleansings.

facts.title: Maya Blue
facts.description: They created a vivid, remarkably durable pigment known as 'Maya Blue.' The exact chemical composition resisted fading for centuries, baffling modern scientists until recently.

source.name: American Chemical Society
source.reference: The Mystery of Maya Blue Pigment

facts.title: The Three Sisters
facts.description: Maya agriculture relied heavily on the 'Three Sisters' method of companion planting: growing corn, beans, and squash together so the plants benefited from each other.

source.name: USDA National Agricultural Library
source.reference: The Three Sisters of Indigenous American Agriculture

facts.title: Cyclical Creation
facts.description: According to Maya mythology, the world was created and destroyed multiple times. They believed humans were currently living in the fourth creation, having finally been successfully crafted from maize (corn).

facts.title: The Popol Vuh
facts.description: The Popol Vuh is the most important surviving text of Maya mythology. It details the creation of the world and the epic adventures of the Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque.

source.name: Popol Vuh
source.reference: Translated by Dennis Tedlock, Simon & Schuster

facts.title: Obsidian Blades
facts.description: Because they lacked iron or steel, the Maya used obsidian (volcanic glass) to make weapons and cutting tools. Obsidian blades can have edges much sharper than high-quality steel surgical scalpels.

facts.title: Suturing Wounds
facts.description: Maya healers were advanced in medicine, using human hair to suture wounds and expertly setting broken bones using casts made from plants.

facts.title: The Classic Collapse
facts.description: Between 800 and 1000 CE, the southern lowland Maya cities were mysteriously abandoned. Modern historians attribute this 'collapse' to a combination of severe drought, overpopulation, and relentless warfare.

source.name: NASA
source.reference: The Fall of the Maya: Drought and Climate Change
